channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: The 10 Biggest ChatGPT-5 Problems & How to Fix Them
executive_summary: In the video, Nate B Jones discusses the top 10 problems with ChatGPT-5, highlighting issues like router misrouting and model drift. He emphasizes the importance of targeted prompting and custom instructions to enhance performance, as the new multi-model system often defaults to less capable models, impacting user experience.
bullets: ChatGPT-5 often defaults to a faster but less capable model due to its hidden routing system, which can lead to shallow responses. This is like choosing a quick snack instead of a nutritious meal; you may feel full, but you won't get the nutrients you need. To counter this, users should add explicit prompts like 'think hard' to encourage deeper analysis.

API users can directly select models, while chat users rely on the routing system, which can limit their options. This is akin to a restaurant where some customers can order from the full menu while others are stuck with a limited selection. Pro plans offer some model selection, but for most users, effective prompting is essential to steer the conversation.

Upgrades to GPT-5 can disrupt established workflows, a phenomenon known as model drift. This is like upgrading your favorite software and finding your old files are no longer compatible. To manage this, it's crucial to maintain a version history of prompts and re-test workflows with new models to ensure stability.

Despite larger token windows in GPT-5, perfect recall of context is not guaranteed. This is similar to trying to remember every detail of a long movie without taking notes; some important scenes may slip your mind. Techniques like U-shaped prompting and context reminders are necessary to keep the conversation on track.

GPT-5's smaller variants may return broken JSON or incorrectly claim tool actions, which can lead to confusion. Think of it like a chef misreading a recipe and ending up with a dish that doesn't taste right. To mitigate these errors, users should explicitly request JSON schemas and require proof artifacts for tool calls.

Using reasoning mode in GPT-5 takes longer and consumes more tokens, which can impact efficiency. It's like running a marathon instead of a sprint; while you may gain deeper insights, it requires more time and energy. Users can adjust personality settings to maintain warmth or empathy without always relying on costly deep reasoning.

Scientific queries that approach safety boundaries may trigger conservative completions, leading to guardrail friction. This is like a car hitting a speed bump; it slows you down unexpectedly. Users should be aware of this and monitor their usage, especially on lower-tier plans, to avoid silent downgrades in model quality.

Lower-tier plans can experience silent downgrades after heavy usage, which can degrade the quality of responses. It's like a phone plan that throttles your data after reaching a limit; you may not realize your speed has dropped until it affects your experience. Monitoring usage or switching tiers can help maintain performance.

Nate B Jones emphasizes that 'prompting is a durable skill, and so is evolving your workflows when models change.' This highlights the need for adaptability in the fast-evolving AI landscape, much like a gardener who must adjust their techniques as seasons change to ensure a healthy crop.

The idea that AI will perfectly understand vague English is a misconception. As Nate puts it, 'The magic rocks that think will understand exactly what you want in your vague English is not reality.' Users must provide clear and specific prompts to achieve the desired outcomes.

Maintaining a prompt version history is essential for managing model drift. This is like keeping a diary of your workouts; if you don't track your progress, you may not know what works best for you. Regularly re-testing prompts with new models helps ensure continued effectiveness.

Explicitly requesting proof artifacts when using tool calls can reduce errors in responses. Think of it like asking for a receipt after a purchase; it provides verification and accountability, ensuring that the AI's actions align with user expectations.
key_takeaways: Set explicit prompts like 'think hard' to encourage deeper analysis in ChatGPT-5. This matters because it helps avoid shallow responses that could derail your project. Without this, you risk receiving incomplete or inaccurate information.

Maintain a version history of prompts and re-test workflows with new models to manage model drift effectively. This is crucial because upgrades can disrupt established processes, leading to inefficiencies. If you skip this step, you may find your previous methods no longer work.

Monitor your usage on lower-tier plans to avoid silent downgrades in model quality. This is important because heavy use can lead to unexpected drops in performance. If you don't keep an eye on this, you might end up with subpar responses when you need them most.

Incorporate U-shaped prompting and context reminders to enhance long-context recall in GPT-5. This is vital for maintaining coherence in conversations, especially when dealing with complex topics. Failing to do so can result in lost context and confusion.

Regularly request JSON schemas and proof artifacts when using tool calls to ensure reliability. This is essential for avoiding errors in responses, which can derail your project. If you neglect this practice, you may encounter broken outputs that hinder your progress.

technical_terms.Router Misrouting: A situation where ChatGPT-5 defaults to a faster but less capable model due to its hidden routing system. Think of it like a GPS that takes you on the quickest route, even if it's not the best one.
technical_terms.API: An interface that allows users to interact directly with a software application, enabling model selection and more control. Like a remote control for your TV, it gives you the power to choose what you want to watch.
technical_terms.Model Drift: The phenomenon where upgrades to AI models disrupt established workflows, leading to inconsistencies. Think of it like a river changing course; if you don't adapt, you might find yourself in uncharted waters.
technical_terms.Token Windows: The maximum number of tokens (words or characters) that a model can process at once. Like a suitcase that can only hold a certain amount of clothes; if you try to pack too much, something will spill out.
technical_terms.JSON: A lightweight data interchange format that is easy for humans to read and write, and easy for machines to parse and generate. Think of it like a neatly organized filing cabinet where each document is easy to find.
technical_terms.Reasoning Mode: A setting in GPT-5 that allows for deeper analysis but takes longer and uses more tokens. Like a detective taking time to gather all clues before solving a case; thoroughness comes at a cost.
technical_terms.Guardrail Friction: The resistance encountered when scientific queries approach safety boundaries, leading to conservative responses. It's like driving a car too close to the edge of a cliff; you may get slowed down for safety.
technical_terms.Silent Downgrade: A situation where lower-tier plans experience a drop in model quality without any notification. Think of it like a subscription service that quietly reduces your access without telling you.
technical_terms.Custom Instructions: User-defined settings that tailor the AI's responses to better fit individual needs. Like a tailor adjusting a suit to fit perfectly; it makes the experience more comfortable and effective.
technical_terms.U-shaped Prompting: A technique that involves structuring prompts in a way that helps maintain context over longer interactions. Think of it like a roller coaster that dips down and then rises back up, keeping the thrill alive throughout the ride.
